title: Proverbs 29
subtitle: Stubborn Neck; Righteous Rule; Wise Servant; Correction and Rest

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — Stubborn Neck; Righteous Rule; Wise Servant; Anger vs Self-Control

heb.paragraph: A person who remains stiff-necked (maqšeh ʿōrep) after many rebukes will suddenly be destroyed — without remedy (v. 1). The ʿōrep (back of the neck) image comes from an ox that refuses to turn when the yoke directs it. Stubbornness under correction accumulates consequences until a sudden, irreversible breaking point.

Where there is no ḥāzôn (prophetic vision), the people cast off restraint (v. 18). The word refers specifically to divine revelation communicated through prophets — not generic 'vision statements.' Without God's word actively proclaimed, moral order dissolves. The verb yippāraʿ means to let loose, run wild — the image is of a people unrestrained because unleadered.

panels.ctx: The first half of ch.29 addresses governance and social order: stubborn resistance to correction (v.1), the rejoicing of a people under righteous rule (v.2), the king who loves justice (v.4), and the contrast between the hot-tempered fool and the self-controlled wise person (v.11).

cross.note: Moses saw that the people had broken loose — 'pāraʿ' — the same word as v.18's "cast off restraint." Without the word of God, the people break loose as Israel did at Sinai.
Righteousness exalts a nation — the same social-righteousness principle as the rejoicing under righteous rule (v.2).

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stiff-necked after many rebukes -- Alter notes the cumulative nature of the warning: not one rebuke refused but many. The resistance to correction hardens over time into a structural condition.

A fool gives full vent to their rage, but a wise person quietly holds it back -- Alter notes the contrast between uncontrolled emotional expression (full vent) and the wisdom of regulation. Emotional intelligence is a wisdom practice.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Whoever remains stiff-necked after many rebukes will suddenly be destroyed -- Waltke notes the 'suddenly' mirrors the earlier wisdom warnings. The person who refuses cumulative correction forfeits the gradual path and faces abrupt consequence.

Where there is no vision the people perish; but blessed is the one who heeds wisdom's instruction -- Waltke notes chazon (vision/revelation) is prophetic revelation from God. Absence of prophetic revelation produces social disorder (yipara, runs wild). The verse is about authoritative divine word, not personal goal-setting.
Calvin: Whoever is often reproved but stiffens his neck will suddenly be broken -- the increasing resistance to correction produces an increasingly severe eventual judgment.

Calvin: Where there is no vision the people perish -- this is about the absence of God's word. When prophetic revelation is absent, social order breaks down. The verse is not about personal ambition but about the communal necessity of divine revelation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the stiffened neck (qashah oref) -- the standard Hebrew idiom for stubborn resistance to correction, applied to Israel throughout the OT (Exod 32:9; Deut 9:6; Isa 48:4).
NET: chazon (prophetic vision/revelation) -- not a personal vision or goal but authoritative divine revelation. The note distinguishes this from modern self-help applications of the verse.

sections.header: Verses 15–27 — Correction and Rest; Where There Is No Vision; Fear of Man Is a Snare

heb.paragraph: The fear of man brings a môqēš — a snare (v. 25). The word describes a bird trap or animal snare: once caught, the victim is immobilised. People-pleasing is not a personality trait but a spiritual trap that freezes moral action. The antidote is trust in the LORD, which sets one on high (yĕśuggāb) — elevated above the reach of human approval.

The unjust person is a tôʿăbâ (abomination) to the righteous, and the upright in his way is a tôʿăbâ to the wicked (v. 27). The final verse of the Solomonic collection reveals an irreconcilable mutual revulsion: each side finds the other's way of life repulsive. There is no neutral ground. The wisdom tradition ends its proverbs with a stark either/or.

panels.ctx: The second half contains the chapter's most famous verse (v.18 — where there is no vision) alongside the fear-of-man snare (v.25), and the chapter's closing observation: the LORD's judgments are compared to those of human authorities. The fear of the LORD overcomes the fear of man.

cross.note: Whoever fears the LORD has a secure fortress — the fear-of-man snare (v.25) is the negative counterpart to the fear-of-the-LORD security.
We must obey God rather than human beings — the NT application of v.25's trust-in-the-LORD over fear-of-man principle.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rod and reproof give wisdom, but a child left to himself disgraces his mother — the paired instruments of discipline (rod and reproof, physical correction and verbal instruction) work together. Alter notes the child left entirely to his own inclinations does not merely fail to become wise — he becomes a source of shame to the parent who abdicated the disciplinary responsibility.

The righteous detest the dishonest; the wicked detest the upright -- Alter notes the mutual-detesting observation closes ch.29 with the sharpest statement of the two-ways antithesis. The righteous and wicked are not merely different; they are structurally repellent to each other.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A rod and a reproof give wisdom, but a child left to himself disgraces his mother -- Waltke notes the parental discipline pair (rod + reproof) works through both physical correction and verbal instruction together.

Fear of man will prove to be a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe -- Waltke notes the man-fearing/LORD-trusting antithesis is one of Proverbs' most practically useful. The fear of human opinion is specifically named as a trap.
Calvin: A child left to himself brings shame -- the parental abdication of discipline is not kindness but dereliction. The undisciplined child develops a character that ultimately shames the parent.
Calvin: The fear of man is a snare -- no analysis of temptation is more accurate. The fear of what others will think is one of the most powerful forces that prevents covenant faithfulness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on rod and reproof together discusses how the two instruments of correction function in tandem throughout the Proverbs rod-passages. The physical correction (shevet) addresses the body; the reproof (tokhachah) addresses the mind and will. Both are required because character formation is both somatic and rational — neither alone is sufficient.

NET: fear of man (cherdath adam) -- the note traces the man-fearing trap through the OT. The person whose behaviour is primarily governed by human opinion has made a fundamental epistemological error about who ultimately matters.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Where there is no revelation, people cast off restraint; but blessed is the one who heeds wisdom's instruction."NoteḤāzôn — prophetic vision, the word of God. Without it, community becomes ungovernable. With wisdom's instruction, blessing follows.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Where There Is No Vision
rows.text: Where there is no revelation, people cast off restraint; but blessed is the one who heeds wisdom's instruction. Absence of divine revelation produces social disorder. The verse is about the communal necessity of authoritative divine word.

rows.label: Fear of Man as Snare
rows.text: Fear of man will prove to be a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe. The fear of human opinion is specifically named as a trap. Whose opinion ultimately matters determines what kind of person you become.

tx.title: Chapter 29 — end of the Hezekian collection
tx.content: Chapter 29 concludes the Hezekian collection (25–29). MT and LXX diverge on several individual proverbs, with the LXX adding material in some verses and condensing in others. The chapter's final verse ('Many seek an audience with a ruler') is rendered differently in LXX, which emphasizes divine rather than royal favor.
tx.note: The shift from royal favor (MT) to divine favor (LXX) at the collection's conclusion may reflect the translator's editorial decision to end on a theological rather than political note.

debate.title: Proverbs 29:18 and Personal Vision: A Misapplication?

positions.name: About prophetic revelation, not personal goals
positions.argument: Chazon is consistently used for prophetic/divine revelation throughout the OT (Isa 1:1; Ezek 12:22-24; Dan 8:1). The verse addresses the communal need for authoritative divine word, not individual goal-setting.

positions.name: Can be legitimately extended to personal vision
positions.argument: While the primary meaning is prophetic revelation, the principle that purposeful direction from God prevents aimlessness can be legitimately applied to the individual level.
